Netflix Data Exploration Business Case Study¶

Suhel Mandal¶

In [ ]:

1. Importing Libraries¶

In [ ]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

2. Loading the Dataset¶

In [ ]:
!wget "https://d2beiqkhq929f0.cloudfront.net/public_assets/assets/000/000/940/original/netflix.csv" -O netflix.csv

--2026-02-22 03:20:39--  https://d2beiqkhq929f0.cloudfront.net/public_assets/assets/000/000/940/original/netflix.csv

In [ ]:
df = pd.read_csv('/content/netflix.csv')
In [ ]:
print(f'   Rows    : {df.shape[0]}')
print(f'   Columns : {df.shape[1]}')

   Rows    : 8807
   Columns : 12

From the shape, we find out that there are: 8807 rows and 12 columns in the dataset.

Goal: Analyzing Netflix data to help the Business Decide:¶

  1. To identify the content type
  2. Determining the content ratings
  3. Geographical distribution of data
  4. Analysing patterns of releases
  5. Analysing top directors and actors
  6. Optimising content

Data understanding & exploration is important as we need to know the data before working on it.

4. Checking for Missing Values¶

In [ ]:
missing_value_count = df.isnull().sum()
missing_value_percent = 100 * df.isnull().sum() / len(df)

missing_table = pd.DataFrame({'Missing Count'  : missing_value_count, 'Missing Percent': missing_value_percent.round(2)})
print('Columns with missing values:')
print(missing_table[missing_table['Missing Count'] > 0])

Columns with missing values:
            Missing Count  Missing Percent
director             2634            29.91
cast                  825             9.37
country               831             9.44
date_added             10             0.11
rating                  4             0.05
duration                3             0.03

From the above table, we see that the there are many missing values for the directors. Netflix should work on it and find the directors to give them a better recognition.

5. Handelling Missing Values¶

We fill in missing values so they don't cause errors in our analysis.

In [ ]:
df['director'] = df['director'].fillna('Unknown Director')
df['cast'] = df['cast'].fillna('Unknown Cast')
df['country'] = df['country'].fillna('Unknown Country')
df['date_added'] = df['date_added'].fillna('Unknown Date')
df['rating'] = df['rating'].fillna('Unknown Rating')
df['duration'] = df['duration'].fillna('Unknown Duration')
In [ ]:
print(f'Missing Value Counts after handeling them: \n{df.isnull().sum()}')

Missing Value Counts after handeling them: 
show_id         0
type            0
title           0
director        0
cast            0
country         0
date_added      0
release_year    0
rating          0
duration        0
listed_in       0
description     0
dtype: int64

6. Converting Categorical Columns¶

We convert text columns to 'category' type — this saves memory and helps with analysis

In [ ]:
df['type'] = df['type'].astype('category')
df['rating'] = df['rating'].astype('category')
df['listed_in'] = df['listed_in'].astype('category')

print('The required columns has been converted to category')
df[['type','rating','listed_in']].dtypes

The required columns has been converted to category
Out[ ]:
0
type category
rating category
listed_in category

7. Creating New Date Columns¶

We extract useful information from the 'date_added' column like month, year, and week.

In [ ]:
df['date_added'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date_added'], errors='coerce')

df['month_added'] = df['date_added'].dt.month
df['year_added'] = df['date_added'].dt.year
df['week'] = df['date_added'].dt.isocalendar().week

print('Movies/TV-Shows')
df['type'].value_counts()

Just by value_counts() function, we see the count of each category in descending order. The movies dominate the Netflix platform with mature rating as majority.

In [ ]:
print('Unique Value Counts')
print(f'Unique Title: {df['title'].nunique()}')
print(f'Unique Director: {df['director'].nunique()}')
print(f'Unique Cast: {df['cast'].nunique()}')
print(f'Unique Country: {df['country'].nunique()}')
print(f'Unique Genre: {df['listed_in'].nunique()}')
print(f'Release Year Range: {df['release_year'].min()} to {df['release_year'].max()}')

Unique Value Counts
Unique Title: 8807
Unique Director: 4529
Unique Cast: 7693
Unique Country: 749
Unique Genre: 514
Release Year Range: 1925 to 2021

The nunique() function the number of unique vales of each category. In the abovw table, e can see that the unique values of each category or colums has been ruled out and displayed for analysis.

9. Un-nesting Comma Seperated Values¶

Columns like 'country', 'cast', 'director' have multiple values in one cell. We are splitting them into separate rows so we can count them properly.

In [ ]:
# Un-nest Country
df_country = df.copy()
df_country = df_country.assign(country=df_country['country'].str.split(',')) \
                 .explode('country')
df_country['country'] = df_country['country'].str.strip()

# Un-nest Cast
df_cast = df.copy()
df_cast = df_cast.assign(cast=df_cast['cast'].str.split(',')) \
                  .explode('cast')
df_cast['cast'] = df_cast['cast'].str.strip()

# Un-nest Director
df_director = df.copy()
df_director = df_director.assign(director=df_director['director'].str.split(',')) \
                          .explode('director')
df_director['director'] = df_director['director'].str.strip()

# Un-nest Genre (listed_in)
df_genre = df.copy()
df_genre = df_genre.assign(listed_in=df_genre['listed_in'].astype(str).str.split(',')) \
                    .explode('listed_in')
df_genre['listed_in'] = df_genre['listed_in'].str.strip()


print('Un-nesting has been done:')
print(f'df_country rows : {df_country.shape[0]}')
print(f'df_cast    rows : {df_cast.shape[0]}')
print(f'df_director rows: {df_director.shape[0]}')
print(f'df_genre   rows : {df_genre.shape[0]}')

Un-nesting has been done:
df_country rows : 10850
df_cast    rows : 64951
df_director rows: 9612
df_genre   rows : 19323

10.Visual Analysis — Movies vs TV Shows¶

10.1 Count Plot and Pie-chart¶

In [ ]:
type_count = df['type'].value_counts()
In [ ]:
plt.subplot(2,3,1)
sns.countplot(data = df, x = 'type', hue = 'type')

plt.subplot(2,3,3)
plt.pie(type_count, labels = type_count.index, startangle = 90 ,autopct = '%1.1f%%', explode = (0.1,0))
plt.title('Movies vs TV Shows')

plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))

print(type_count,'\n')
print('Netflix has around 70% Movies and 30% of TV shows \nClearly Movies dominate the platform \nNetflix should consider adding more TV Shows to keep a balance')

Netflix Business Insight

type
Movie      6131
TV Show    2676
Name: count, dtype: int64 

Netflix has around 70% Movies and 30% of TV shows 
Clearly Movies dominate the platform 
Netflix should consider adding more TV Shows to keep a balance

10.2 Content added Each Year¶

In [ ]:
# Grouping By Year Data and counting the titles

year_data = df[df['year_added'] >= 2010].groupby(['year_added', 'type']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

# Convert the 'year_data' from wide to long format for seaborn
year_data_melted = year_data.reset_index().melt(id_vars='year_added', var_name='type', value_name='count')

# Now use sns.barplot with the long-format DataFrame
sns.barplot(data=year_data_melted, x='year_added', y='count', hue='type')
plt.title('Number of Titles Added to Netflix Each Year')
plt.xlabel('Year Added')
plt.ylabel('Number of Titles')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print('It is seen that, Netflix rapidly grew its content library from 2016 to 2019.')
print('TV Shows have been growing faster in recent years.')

/tmp/ipython-input-1569498668.py:3: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  year_data = df[df['year_added'] >= 2010].groupby(['year_added', 'type']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

Netflix Business Insight

It is seen that, Netflix rapidly grew its content library from 2016 to 2019.
TV Shows have been growing faster in recent years.

11. Rating Distribution¶

In [ ]:
rating_counts = df['rating'].value_counts().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.countplot(data = df, x = 'rating', hue = 'type', order=rating_counts.index)
plt.title('Distribution of Ratings by Content Type')
plt.xlabel('Rating')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print('From the analysis, we can see that TV-MA (Mature Audience) and TV-14 dominate the platform.')
print('Netflix focuses mostly on adult and teen audiences.')

Netflix Business Insight

From the analysis, we can see that TV-MA (Mature Audience) and TV-14 dominate the platform.
Netflix focuses mostly on adult and teen audiences.

12. Movie Duration Analysis¶

In [ ]:
movies = df[df['type'] == 'Movie']
movies['duration_minutes'] = movies['duration'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(float)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.histplot(data=movies, x='duration_minutes', bins=30, kde=True)
plt.title('Histogram of Movie Duration', fontsize = 14 ,fontweight = 'bold')
plt.xlabel('Duration(minutes)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(f'Average movie duration : {movies["duration_minutes"].mean():.0f} minutes')
print(f'Most common duration   : {movies["duration_minutes"].mode()[0]:.0f} minutes')
print('From the analysis, it is seen that most movies are between 80-120 minutes.')

/tmp/ipython-input-1893610840.py:2: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  movies['duration_minutes'] = movies['duration'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(float)

Netflix Business Insight

Average movie duration : 100 minutes
Most common duration   : 90 minutes
From the analysis, it is seen that most movies are between 80-120 minutes.

13. TV Show Duration Analysis¶

In [ ]:
tv_shows = df[df['type'] == 'TV Show'].copy()
tv_shows.loc[:, 'seasons'] = tv_shows['duration'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(float)

season_counts = tv_shows['seasons'].value_counts().sort_index()

# Converting the season_counts Series to a DataFrame for plotting with seaborn
season_df = season_counts.reset_index()
season_df.columns = ['Number of Seasons', 'Number of TV Shows']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data=season_df, x='Number of Seasons', y='Number of TV Shows', palette='Blues_r')
plt.title('TV Shows: Number of Seasons', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Number of Seasons')
plt.ylabel('Number of TV Shows')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print('Most TV Shows on Netflix have only 1 season.')
print('Maybe Netflix prefers short, limited-run series.')

/tmp/ipython-input-1025299723.py:11: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(data=season_df, x='Number of Seasons', y='Number of TV Shows', palette='Blues_r')

Netflix Business Insight

Most TV Shows on Netflix have only 1 season.
Maybe Netflix prefers short, limited-run series.

14. Release Year by Type¶

In [ ]:
df_release_year = df[df['release_year']>1990]
sns.boxplot(data=df_release_year, x='type' , y='release_year', hue = 'type')
plt.title('Release Year Distribution by Content Type', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Type')
plt.ylabel('Release Year')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print('TV Shows tend to have more recent release years than Movies.')
print('Netflix is adding newer TV Shows compared to Movies \nas TV shows have become more popular these days.')

Netflix Business Insight

TV Shows tend to have more recent release years than Movies.
Netflix is adding newer TV Shows compared to Movies 
as TV shows have become more popular these days.

15. Top 10 Countries for movies and TV Shows¶

15.1 Top 10 Countries for Movies¶

In [ ]:
df_country_movies = df_country[df_country['type'] == 'Movie']

top_10_countries_movies = df_country_movies.groupby('country')['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending= False).head(10).reset_index()
top_10_countries_movies.columns = ['Country','Number of Movies']

plt.figure(figsize=(16, 6))
sns.barplot(data=top_10_countries_movies, x = 'Country', y = 'Number of Movies', palette = 'Reds_r')
plt.title('Top 10 Countries for Movies', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Country')
plt.ylabel('Number of Movies')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(top_10_countries_movies.to_string(index=False))
print('Insight: \nUSA leads in Movies, followed by India and UK.')

/tmp/ipython-input-1537180454.py:7: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(data=top_10_countries_movies, x = 'Country', y = 'Number of Movies', palette = 'Reds_r')

Netflix Business Insight

        Country  Number of Movies
  United States              2752
          India               962
 United Kingdom               534
Unknown Country               440
         Canada               319
         France               303
        Germany               182
          Spain               171
          Japan               119
          China               114
Insight: 
USA leads in Movies, followed by India and UK.

15.2 Top 10 Countries for TV Shows¶

In [ ]:
df_country_tvshows = df_country[df_country['type'] == 'TV Show']

top_10_countries_tvshows = df_country_tvshows.groupby('country')['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending= False).head(10).reset_index()
top_10_countries_tvshows.columns = ['Country','Number of TV Shows']

plt.figure(figsize=(11, 5))
sns.barplot(data=top_10_countries_tvshows, x = 'Number of TV Shows',y = 'Country', palette = 'Blues_r')
plt.title('Top 10 Countries for TV Shows', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Number of TV Shows')
plt.ylabel('Country')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(top_10_countries_tvshows.to_string(index=False))
print('\nUSA dominates the TV Shows section followed by United Kingdom. \nSouth Korea and Japan are rising due to K-dramas and anime.')

16. Best Month to add Content¶

16.1 Best Month to add Movie¶

In [ ]:
# Movies
month_count = df.groupby(['month_added','type'])['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending = False)

month_count = month_count.reset_index()
month_count.columns = ['Month','Type','Count of Content']

movie = month_count[month_count['Type'] == 'Movie']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data = month_count[month_count['Type']=='Movie'], x = 'Month', y = 'Count of Content', palette = 'Spectral')
plt.style.use('default')
plt.title('Best Month to Add Movies', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Count of Movies')
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(movie.to_string(index=False))
print('\nJanuary, April,July and December show the most content additions for Movies.')
print('These align with New Year and Summer viewing peaks.')

/tmp/ipython-input-1838171831.py:2: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  month_count = df.groupby(['month_added','type'])['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending = False)
/tmp/ipython-input-1838171831.py:10: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(data = month_count[month_count['Type']=='Movie'], x = 'Month', y = 'Count of Content', palette = 'Spectral')

Netflix Business Insight

 Month  Type  Count of Content
   7.0 Movie               565
   4.0 Movie               550

   3.0 Movie               529

January, April,July and December show the most content additions for Movies.
These align with New Year and Summer viewing peaks.

16.2 Best Month to add TV Show¶

In [ ]:
# TV Shows
month_count = df.groupby(['month_added','type'])['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending = False)

month_count = month_count.reset_index()
month_count.columns = ['Month','Type','Count of Content']

tvshow = month_count[month_count['Type'] == 'TV Show']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data = month_count[month_count['Type']=='TV Show'], x = 'Month', y = 'Count of Content', palette = 'blend:#7AB,#EDA')
plt.title('Best Month to Add TV Shows', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Count of TV Shows')
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))

print(tvshow.to_string(index=False))
print('\nJune, July, September and December show the most content additions for TV Shows.')
print('These align with New Year and Summer viewing peaks.')

17. Best Week to add Content¶

17.1 Best week to add Movies¶

In [ ]:
week_count = df.groupby(['week','type'])['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending = False)

week_count = week_count.reset_index()
week_count.columns = ['Week','Type','Count of Content']

week_count = week_count.sort_values(by = 'Week')


movie_week = week_count[week_count['Type'] == 'Movie']



plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.lineplot(data = movie_week, x ='Week' ,y = 'Count of Content', color = 'indigo')
plt.fill_between(movie_week['Week'],
                 movie_week['Count of Content'],
                 alpha=0.2, color = 'blue')
plt.title('Best Week to Add Movies', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Week')
plt.ylabel('Count of Movies')
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))

print(movie_week.to_string(index=False))
print('\nMovie releases show clear seasonal clustering, with Week 1 and Week 44 being peak addition periods. \nThis indicates a strategic alignment with high-viewership seasons such as New Year and pre-holiday periods. \nNetflix should continue launching high-budget and flagship movies during these weeks to maximize engagement and subscription growth.')

17.2 Best week to add TV Shows¶

In [ ]:
week_count = df.groupby(['week','type'])['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending = False)

week_count = week_count.reset_index()
week_count.columns = ['Week','Type','Count of Content']

week_count = week_count.sort_values(by = 'Week')


tvshow_week = week_count[week_count['Type'] == 'TV Show']



plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.lineplot(data = tvshow_week, x ='Week' ,y = 'Count of Content', color = 'navy')
plt.fill_between(tvshow_week['Week'],
                 tvshow_week['Count of Content'],
                 alpha=0.2, color = 'navy')
plt.title('Best Week to Add TV Shows', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Week')
plt.ylabel('Count of TV Shows')
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(tvshow_week.to_string(index=False))
print('\nTV Show additions show clear weekly clustering rather than uniform distribution. \nThis indicates a strategic release approach aligned with seasonal demand patterns \nsuch as holidays and mid-year breaks. Netflix should continue leveraging historically \nhigh-engagement weeks to launch flagship TV shows to maximize \nviewership and subscriber retention.')

18. Top 10 Directors¶

In [119]:
top_10_directors = df_director[df_director['director']!= 'Unknown Director'].groupby('director')['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending= False).head(10).reset_index()
top_10_directors.columns = ['Director','Number of Titles']

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
sns.barplot(data=top_10_directors, x = 'Director', y = 'Number of Titles', palette = 'rocket')
plt.title('Top 10 Directors', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Directors')
plt.ylabel('Number of Titles')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(top_10_directors.to_string(index=False))
print('\nThe top directors analysis reveals that Netflix heavily collaborates with regional filmmakers and comedy-special directors. \nThis suggests a dual strategy of producing cost-effective, high-engagement content such as stand-up specials, while also \npartnering with prestigious global directors to strengthen brand positioning. Netflix should continue building long-term \npartnerships with high-performing regional directors to drive localized subscriber growth.')

The top directors analysis reveals that Netflix heavily collaborates with regional filmmakers and comedy-special directors. 
This suggests a dual strategy of producing cost-effective, high-engagement content such as stand-up specials, while also 
partnering with prestigious global directors to strengthen brand positioning. Netflix should continue building long-term 
partnerships with high-performing regional directors to drive localized subscriber growth.

In [123]:
top_10_actors = df_cast[df_cast['cast']!= 'Unknown Cast'].groupby('cast')['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending= False).head(10).reset_index()
top_10_actors.columns = ['Actors','Number of Titles']

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
sns.barplot(data=top_10_actors, x = 'Actors', y = 'Number of Titles', palette = 'flare_r')
plt.title('Top 10 Actors', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Actors')
plt.ylabel('Number of Titles')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(top_10_actors.to_string(index=False))

print('\nThe top actors analysis reveals a strong dominance of Indian and Japanese talent, indicating Netflix’s \nstrategic focus on regional markets such as India and Japan. The frequent presence of veteran actors \nsuggests that Netflix leverages star power and licensed back-catalog content to drive engagement. \nNetflix should continue investing in regional superstars and animation content to strengthen its global \nexpansion strategy.')

Netflix should continue investing in regional superstars and animation content to strengthen its global 
expansion strategy.

20. Genre Analysis¶

In [125]:
top_genres = df_genre['listed_in'].value_counts().head(10).reset_index()
top_genres.columns = ['Genre', 'Count']

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(data=top_genres, x='Count', y='Genre', palette='Reds_r')
plt.title('Top 15 Genres on Netflix', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Count')
plt.ylabel('Genre')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print(top_genres.to_string(index=False))
print('\nGenre analysis reveals that International Movies and Dramas dominate Netflix’s content library, \nhighlighting the company’s strong global expansion and localization strategy. The high presence \nof Comedy and Documentary content indicates a balanced portfolio approach aimed at maximizing \nengagement across diverse audience segments. Netflix should continue investing in international and \ndrama-based content to sustain global subscriber growth.')

Genre analysis reveals that International Movies and Dramas dominate Netflix’s content library, 
highlighting the company’s strong global expansion and localization strategy. The high presence 
of Comedy and Documentary content indicates a balanced portfolio approach aimed at maximizing 
engagement across diverse audience segments. Netflix should continue investing in international and 
drama-based content to sustain global subscriber growth.

21. Time Gap¶

In [126]:
df['time_gap'] = df['year_added'] - df['release_year']

content_time_gap = df.groupby('time_gap')['title'].nunique().sort_values(ascending= False).reset_index()
content_time_gap.columns = ['Time Gap', 'Number of Content']

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

sns.histplot(data=df, x='time_gap', bins=20, kde=True)

plt.title('Distribution of Time Gap Between Release & Netflix Addition',
          fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Time Gap (Years)')
plt.ylabel('Count of Content')

plt.show()

from IPython.display import HTML
display(HTML("<h2 style='color:#E50914;'>Netflix Business Insight</h2>"))
print('\nThe time gap analysis shows that most content is added to Netflix within a few years of release, \nindicating an aggressive acquisition strategy focused on fresh content. A significant number of titles \nwith zero time gap suggest strong investment in Netflix Originals. Additionally, the presence of older \ntitles reflects a catalog expansion strategy aimed at maintaining content diversity and depth.')

Netflix Business Insight

The time gap analysis shows that most content is added to Netflix within a few years of release, 
indicating an aggressive acquisition strategy focused on fresh content. A significant number of titles 
with zero time gap suggest strong investment in Netflix Originals. Additionally, the presence of older 
titles reflects a catalog expansion strategy aimed at maintaining content diversity and depth.

Insights Summary¶

  1. Netflix has ~70% Movies and ~30% TV Shows, but TV Shows are growing faster.

  2. USA dominates both Movies and TV Shows. India is #2 for Movies — a key growth market.

  3. South Korea and Japan are rising for TV Shows (K-dramas, Anime), showing strong global demand for Asian content.

  4. TV-MA and TV-14 ratings dominate — Netflix primarily targets adults and teens.

  5. January and July are the best months to add content (peak engagement periods).

  6. International Movies, Dramas, and Comedies are the most common genres — reflecting a global and mass-appeal strategy.

  7. Most movies are 80–120 minutes long — optimized for standard viewer attention span.

  8. Most TV Shows have only 1 season — indicating preference for limited series or experimental formats.

  9. Many titles are added to Netflix within 1 year of release — showing an aggressive, fresh-content acquisition strategy.

  10. Netflix follows a hybrid model: fast licensing + strong Originals + deep catalog of older titles.

Recommendations for Netflix¶

  1. Invest in Indian content — India is #2 in movies. Expand Bollywood and regional language originals to accelerate subscriber growth.

  2. Expand Korean & Japanese TV Shows — K-dramas and Anime have massive global fanbases and strong binge potential.

  3. Produce more family content — TV-G and TV-Y categories are underrepresented. This is an untapped growth opportunity.

  4. Focus on limited series (1–2 seasons) — audiences prefer short, complete, binge-worthy stories.

  5. Launch high-budget Originals in January & July — these months show peak engagement.

  6. Increase investment in International Dramas — drama drives watch-time and retention globally.

  7. Invest in Documentaries and Stand-up Comedy — low production cost, strong niche loyalty, high ROI.

  8. Reduce time gap further — license movies within 12 months of release to meet audience expectations for fresh content.

  9. Build long-term partnerships with high-output directors and creators — consistent collaboration ensures steady content flow.

  10. Strengthen franchise-based storytelling — multi-season universes improve retention and brand loyalty.
